narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading to the rejection. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like academic rankings, test scores, and background that best signal bar exam success. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which was the top concern because it placed you at the bottom of your class that year, far below the average 5th or 6th decile—pushing the prediction firmly toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by showing stronger performance than most applicants, helping pull the odds slightly toward passing. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 1st also played a role, similarly signaling lower early performance compared to peers and nudging the model toward failure, though less intensely than your later-year standing. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 33 (below the 36.7 average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—which is common for over 90% of applicants—factored in but weren't the main drivers here. In essence, while your college grades were a bright spot, your consistently low decile rankings in school suggested to the model a risk of struggling on the high-stakes bar exam, unlike typical admitted students who rank higher across the board. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant with clear strengths; it's the model highlighting patterns where early and mid-undergrad deciles like yours correlated with bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, seeking ways to improve your class rankings through targeted study or transfers if possible, and applying to programs with holistic reviews that weigh your full story beyond these metrics. Building a stronger narrative around your GPA and any extracurriculars could also help in appeals or alternative schools. With focused effort, you can shift these odds in your favor for the next round.
